Title: Johor allocates RM110,000 to upgrade teachers' rooms in Tanjung Surat schools
Article:
KOTA TINGGI: The Johor government has continued its support for educators with a RM110,000 allocation for teachers' rooms under the Tanjung Surat state constituency, in a move aimed at improving workplace comfort and teaching conditions.
A total of 22 schools received RM5,000 each to upgrade teachers' facilities at an event held at Dewan Bestari Felda Adela here today.
Johor Education and Information Committee chairman Aznan Tamin said the assistance went beyond financial aid, describing it as recognition of educators' sacrifices in shaping future generations.
Aznan, who is also the Tanjung Surat assemblyman, said the allocation symbolised appreciation for the dedication of teachers who work tirelessly every day.
He added that improving teachers' rooms would directly enhance the working environment, which in turn supports better teaching quality in schools.
At the state level, the Johor government has allocated RM5.975 million under the programme, benefiting 1,195 schools, including government-aided religious schools as well as Chinese and Tamil vernacular schools. More than 49,000 teachers statewide are expected to benefit.
Each school receives RM5,000 in phases for upgrading teachers' rooms, including essential items such as air-conditioning units, chairs, desks, sofas and other facilities aimed at improving comfort and morale.
Aznan said the initiative, which has been implemented consistently since 2022, reflected the Johor government's sustained focus on education welfare.
He said the programme was designed to ensure teachers were better supported in their daily responsibilities while fostering a more conducive school environment.
"This effort is ongoing, not seasonal. It is part of our long-term commitment to empower educators and strengthen the quality of education in Johor," he said.
The initiative is expected to further boost teachers' morale and contribute to the development of well-rounded, competitive and values-driven students in the state.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Tioman tourism hit; BN rep asks state govt to waive ferry fuel surcharge
Article:
ROMPIN: A Barisan Nasional lawmaker has voiced concern about slowing visitor numbers to Pulau Tioman since the ferry fuel surcharge came into effect on April 20.
Tioman assemblyman Datuk Seri Mohd Johari Hussain urged the Pahang government to step in with solutions to ensure that tourist arrivals to the duty-free island remain unaffected.
"I view the fuel surcharge as an additional cost for travellers, on top of the ticket price ranging between RM55 and RM60 one way. It will be a burden, especially for large families or big groups.
"Exemptions from the ferry fuel surcharge to the island would help boost visitor arrivals and encourage more people to travel domestically," he said when contacted today.
Johari said there were initially talks earlier this month about imposing a RM16 fuel surcharge on all passengers, including Malaysians, travelling to Pulau Tioman, but the plan did not materialise following objections from various quarters.
"Then, the Transport Ministry introduced a targeted nationwide fuel surcharge mechanism. Perhaps the state government could implement measures to absorb the additional cost," he said.
Last week, a resort owner on Pulau Tioman said post-monsoon visitor arrivals have been sluggish amid fuel price uncertainty, warning that the timing of the
tiered ferry fuel surcharge
could disrupt travel plans.
Meanwhile, Pahang Unity, Tourism and Culture Committee chairman Leong Yu Man said the state government is studying suitable measures to cushion the impact of the fuel surcharge but declined to elaborate further.
The Penang Port Commission and Penang Port Sdn Bhd have announced their decision
not to impose a fuel surcharge on ferry services
, despite the government introducing a nationwide fuel surcharge mechanism on April 20.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Counterfeit medical gear syndicate smashed; goods worth RM1.34mil seized in raid
Article:
JOHOR BARU: The Domestic Trade and Cost of Living Ministry crippled a suspected counterfeit health equipment syndicate following a raid on a warehouse and packing centre here, seizing goods worth nearly RM1.34 million.
The suspect, a woman, is set to be charged at the Sessions Court here tomorrow over their alleged involvement in the counterfeit health equipment case.
In a joint operation in last month, enforcement officers from Putrajaya and Johor, moved in on a premises believed to be a storage and distribution hub for online sales of health screening devices bearing unauthorised trademarks.
The raid, conducted under the Trademarks Act, was carried out with a warrant and in the presence of representatives of the trademark owner, following complaints over suspected fake products being sold in the market.
Domestic Trade and Cost of Living Ministry enforcement director-general Datuk Azman Adam said inspections uncovered a wide range of medical devices suspected to be counterfeit and intended for distribution to consumers.
Among the items seized were automatic blood pressure monitors, nebulisers, digital thermometers, weighing scales, pulse oximeters and blood glucose testing devices.
"Preliminary checks indicate that a substantial quantity of these products were using registered trademarks without the authorisation of the legitimate owner," he said in a statement today.
Azman said the total seizure, comprising multiple models of health-related devices, was valued at RM1,333,970.
A woman, who identified herself as the manager of the premises, was detained to assist investigations, with enforcement officers also recording statements and issuing seizure documentation at the scene.
The case is being investigated under the Trademarks Act for the unauthorised use of registered trademarks.
Members of the public can report counterfeit goods and intellectual property violations through official ministry channels, including its WhatsApp hotline at 019848 8000, e-Aduan portal - http://eaduan.kpdn.gov.my; or call centre 1-800-886-800.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Germany approves new rescue bid for stranded whale
Article:
INSEL POEL: German officials today gave the green light for a fresh rescue attempt for a humpback whale which has been stranded off the country's Baltic Sea coast for over a month.
The 13-metre (over 40 foot) whale and its struggle for survival have gripped Germany since the sea mammal beached on a sandbank near the city of Luebeck, far from its natural habitat.
After several initial attempts failed, two entrepreneurs came forward to finance a new rescue bid which will involve loading the cetacean onto a special barge and carrying it out to deeper waters.
Till Backhaus, environment minister for the state of Mecklenburg-Western Pomerania, said that two vets had examined the whale and believed it was "fit to be transported."
After his announcement, rescuers in diving suits could be seen next to the whale, standing in shallow waters near the island of Poel, with the barge close by.
The creature, dubbed "Timmy" by some of the German press, was first spotted stuck on a sandbank on March 23 near Luebeck before freeing itself and then becoming stuck again several times.
At the start of April officials gave up on the animal, saying they believed it could not be saved.
But this triggered an outcry and authorities were persuaded to let the entrepreneurs come up with a rescue plan.
Some scientists have strongly criticised the decision to allow further rescue bids, believing they will be too risky for the whale and estimating the chances of success as low.
Backhaus however insisted it was his "absolute priority" that the mammal be rescued alive, and he was trying to "do everything possible to help" the creature.
The saga has sparked a media frenzy – with non-stop coverage from TV channels, online outlets and social media influencers – but has also led to angry spats and conspiracy theories. - AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Policy reforms needed to tackle Sabah landfill, marine pollution issues
Article:
KOTA KINABALU: Comprehensive impact studies and policy reforms are crucial to addressing critical waste management issues in the Sabah state capital.
Datuk Jasnih Daya (Gagasan-Pantai Dalit) said the 46.5-hectare Kayu Madang landfill, the primary disposal site for the west coast, including Kota Kinabalu, Tuaran, Penampang, Putatan, and Kota Belud, receives about 800 tonnes of solid waste daily.
He said the facility, which replaced the Lokawi landfill in 1996, is nearing its maximum capacity and entering its final operational phase, placing immense strain on the state waste management system.
​​​​​"The existing landfill site must be upgraded to a fully sanitary landfill system with proper gas control, leachate treatment, and systematic daily covering of waste," he said during the debate on the policy speech at the state legislative assembly here.
He said current disposal practices are still largely open or semi-sanitary, leading to persistent odour pollution caused by gases such as methane and hydrogen sulfide.
Weather conditions often carry these odours into nearby residential areas, affecting the quality of life for surrounding communities.
"Mandatory waste segregation must be implemented without delay to reduce the burden on landfill sites," he said, adding that such measures would significantly lower landfill volumes while improving recycling efficiency.
Jasnih urged the state government to plan for long-term solutions, including the development of new disposal sites and the exploration of waste-to-energy technologies.
Linking the issue to downstream impacts, he said poor waste management on land is contributing to growing pollution along the Mengkabung Gayang coastline in Tuaran, where plastic waste is accumulating.
Citing United Nations data, he said between 60 and 80 per cent of marine waste consists of plastic, while around 80 per cent of ocean pollution originates from land-based sources, often carried by rivers into the sea
He added that the situation is further compounded by inconsistent waste collection services in some coastal villages, forcing residents to resort to informal disposal methods such as open burning or dumping waste directly into waterways.
Jasnih said the consequences are already being felt, with fishermen facing higher operational costs due to damaged nets, while microplastics entering the marine food chain pose long-term health risks.
"Failure to act now will not only strain our waste systems but also affect livelihoods, public health and the sustainability of our environment," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Construction company owner held over RM90,000 drone purchase fraud
Article:
ALOR STAR: The Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) has arrested a construction company owner for submitting falsified documents to a financial institution for the purchase of a drone worth RM90,000.
A MACC source said the man, in his 50s, was detained at noon yesterday after giving his statement at the Kedah MACC office.
Preliminary investigations revealed the suspect is believed to have committed the offence in 2024.
He allegedly submitted an invoice to the financial institution, but the drone was never purchased, the source said.
The suspect has been remanded for three days until April 30 to facilitate investigations.
Kedah MACC director Datuk Nazli Rasyid Sulong confirmed the arrest and said the case is being investigated under Section 18 of the MACC Act 2009.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Two company directors jailed, fined RM350,000 over illegal e-waste handling
Article:
SEGAMAT: Two company directors were jailed and fined a total of RM350,000 yesterday after pleading guilty to multiple offences linked to the illegal handling of electronic waste at an industrial site here.
Johor Department of Environment (DoE) director Mohd Famey Yusoff said the convictions marked a significant enforcement action against unlawful recycling activities involving scheduled waste coded SW110 (electrical and electronic waste).
In the Sessions Court here, Teh Choon Kian, 53, director of You Peng (M) Sdn Bhd, pleaded guilty to four charges under the Environmental Quality Act 1974 before Judge Rahimah Abd Majid.
He was sentenced to one day's imprisonment and fined RM100,000 for constructing a recovery plant without Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) approval under Section 34A(6), and a further one day's jail with a RM100,000 fine for placing scheduled waste without approval under Section 34B(1)(a).
Teh was also fined RM50,000 for operating premises without a licence and written permission under Sections 18(1) and 19(1)(a).
The court additionally ordered the forfeiture of 400 metric tonnes of SW110 waste to the government.
In a separate but related case at the same location, transport company Vertex Mission Sdn Bhd and its director, Yeah Hock Heng, were fined RM100,000 after pleading guilty to two charges under Section 18(1A) of the same Act.
The company was found to have used lorries to transport scheduled waste without a valid carrier's licence. The court also ordered the forfeiture of two vehicles and 31 metric tonnes of waste.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Century-old Waterfall Cafe razed after failed relocation talks
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: The Penang government said all negotiation efforts with Waterfall Cafe had failed, with the operator rejecting multiple relocation proposals despite repeated notices to vacate the premises.
This came as enforcement action today saw the demolition of the 103-year-old structure at Taman Botani here.
Chief Minister Chow Kon Yeow said the state had engaged continuously with affected stakeholders, including the cafe operator, in an effort to reach a fair and practical resolution.
"However, all active negotiation channels did not result in any resolution, and the eviction notices issued by the Northeast District Land Administrator since Jan 4, 2024 have not been complied with," he said in a statement.
He said that despite outstanding rental arrears, the state government – through the Northeast District and Land Office – had proposed several alternatives to allow the business to continue operating.
These included two units at a multi-storey car park within a commercial complex linked to the planned Penang Hill cable car project, as well as a temporary relocation site during construction works.
"Unfortunately, these options were rejected without clear justification, even though they were offered in good faith to support business continuity," he added.
Chow said that during a coordination meeting on June 19, 2023, chaired by the then deputy chief minister II, it was agreed that Waterfall Cafe would relocate and settle its rental arrears, but the commitment was not fulfilled.
He said the first eviction notice was issued on Jan 4, 2024, followed by a series of discussions from Feb 19 that year. A final notice was served on April 15 before enforcement action was carried out today.
Separately, former deputy chief minister II Dr P. Ramasamy claimed the enforcement exercise resulted in the demolition of a heritage landmark, describing it as the loss of a historic site.
Ramasamy, who was present at the location with others in an attempt to halt the eviction, said enforcement personnel outnumbered those protesting on site.
He alleged that the entire structure – said to be over a century old and partly recognised for its heritage value – was razed, with only a small Hindu shrine spared.
He also criticised the absence of elected representatives and relevant state agency officials during the operation, calling for assistance to be extended to the displaced operator, including the provision of an alternative site nearby.
The demolition marks the end of one of Penang's long-standing eateries, with its future now clouded by differing accounts over relocation efforts and enforcement compliance.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Establish Sabah east coast energy reserve, Silam assemblyman tells state govt
Article:
KOTA KINABALU: The government should study the need to establish a Sabah energy sovereignty reserve.
Datuk Dr Mohammad Yusof Apdal (Warisan-Silam) said Sabah and Sarawak are major contributors to the nation's oil revenue, yet storage capacity for emergency use remains limited.
"At present, most terminals function primarily for operational and export purposes.
"Establishing a strategic storage zone on Sabah's east coast would reduce the region's exposure to market price fluctuations or global supply disruptions.
"In the event of maritime conflict or disruptions in the Strait of Malacca, Sabah would have its own reserves and could continue operating without interruptions to the supply of petrol, diesel, and industrial fuels," he said when debating the policy speech at the Sabah Legislative Assembly.
Yusof, who is also Lahad Datu member of parliament, said while some argued that it was sufficient to expand the capacity of the Labuan Crude Oil Terminal (LCOT) or existing terminals for reserve storage, Labuan itself is a federal territory and not part of Sabah.
He added that it would be more practical to build a refinery on Sabah's east coast from a logistics cost and operational expenditure perspective.
He said his view is in line with the statement by Sabah Finance Minister Datuk Seri Masidi Manjun that oil exploration is now shifting towards the east coast, including areas in the Sulu Sea and maritime borders near Lahad Datu and Tawau.
'First, it would help narrow the economic gap between Sabah's west and east coasts. Secondly, it would position Lahad Datu or Tawau as new petroleum industry hub capable of attracting foreign direct investment (FDI).
"Thirdly, the establishment of high-value industries on the east coast would indirectly strengthen maritime security control in the EssZone (Eastern Sabah Security Zone) area."
Meanwhile, Datuk Hazem Mubarak Musa (Gagasan-Sungai Manilla) said barter trade is one of the key opportunities that could revive Sandakan's economy.
He added that the system does not only have the capacity to stimulate economic growth but is also capable of attracting investors to Sandakan.
"The economic authorities could impose conditions requiring operators engaged in this business to be based in Sandakan.
"Such activities should be concentrated in the area to help develop the Sandakan Industrial Park. At the same time, this would enable the government to effectively monitor and regulate the business."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: RM2bil sovereign AI cloud to safeguard security, boost talent - PM
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia's RM2 billion investment in a sovereign AI cloud is aimed at strengthening national security, developing local talent, and ensuring the country does not remain only a consumer of global technology, said Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim today.
Speaking at the Temu Anwar session at UCSI University today, Anwar said that while Malaysia maintains an open investment framework and continues to welcome global technology investments, it also has safeguards for national security and data privacy
He was responding to a question from A-Levels student  Thashenndhra Jeyraj, who asked whether the sovereign cloud initiative would meaningfully benefit local startups and small and medium enterprises, or primarily reinforce the dominance of large global technology companies.
Anwar praised the question, saying it reflected a high level of awareness among students on emerging technology policy.
He clarified that Malaysia welcomed major global cloud and technology providers including Google, Microsoft and Amazon Web Services, all of which are expanding their data centre and cloud operations in the country alongside existing investments in Kulim Hi-Tech Park and Johor.
Anwar said the policy reflects Malaysia's approach of maintaining an open digital economy while introducing targeted safeguards for national security and data privacy.
"Matters of security and personal privacy are governed under what is known as the Sovereign Cloud. The Sovereign Cloud is tightly controlled and bounded, with data not permitted to leave the country. For example, military or intelligence secrets are contained within this framework. However, this applies only to a small portion of data.
"It is not about controlling all data," he said.
Anwar also pointed to international regulatory challenges, citing the United States' Cloud Act, which he said allows US authorities to access data held by American companies overseas.
On talent development, the Prime Minister said Malaysia must urgently strengthen its skilled workforce to support rapid growth in artificial intelligence and advanced technologies.
He noted that major investors, including Infineon Technologies, have highlighted the need for higher-skilled engineering talent, prompting closer collaboration between industry and academia, including with Universiti Sains Malaysia.
He said similar partnerships are being expanded across public universities, with all institutions encouraged to establish dedicated artificial intelligence faculties, units or programmes.
"In the fields of IT and AI, we have already started with a faculty at Universiti Teknologi Malaysia (UTM) and now all universities are instructed to establish an AI unit, department or faculty. This is to meet our needs so that we do not remain only users but, as you mentioned, become more skilled leaders in the field,"
Companies such as Huawei, he added, are also being required to provide structured training and high-level exposure for Malaysian students and engineers as part of their investment commitments.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: How should Ukrainian teens committing acts of betrayal be judged?
Article:
AS dusk fell on a crisp September evening in 2024, a group of Ukrainian teenagers huddled beside train tracks near a village in Chernihiv.
The region was under near-constant attack by Russian drones and missiles.  Just two days before, Russia had struck a hospital.
Fifteen-year-old Vitalii pried open the doors of cabinets containing Ukrainian railroad communication and signaling equipment.
According to an indictment filed last March by Chernihiv prosecutors, the boys poured flammable liquid over the cabinets and set them on fire.
They paused to film, doused the flames with water from plastic bottles they had packed, then shared the video clip of the flames with another boy, who forwarded it to a man called "Sania," according to prosecutors and the boys' lawyer.
The man had offered hundreds of dollars online to perform specific tasks – tasks that, Sania neglected to mention, amounted to sabotage against the Ukrainian state.
Since Russia launched its full-scale invasion of Ukraine in 2022, more than 1,100 Ukrainians have been accused of committing arson, terrorism or sabotage in betrayal of their country, according to Ukraine's security service, the SBU.
One in five have been minors. Since the war's start, roughly half of the minors accused of betraying their country have been convicted, while half have been acquitted, freed on bail or sentenced to community service, according to Ukraine's justice ministry.
The minors are usually recruited online by strangers using aliases, mostly assumed by Ukrainian investigators to be working for Russia's special services.
Reuters followed the case of Vitalii and his friends for a year and reviewed nearly a hundred pages of court documents to understand how Ukrainian authorities contend with the larger questions posed by this growing group of young people:
What does justice look like for minors induced to betray their country? Can they return to a society battered by four years of war? And what does Ukraine owe these children who grew up in wartime?
Vitalii pocketed the equivalent of US$23 for his role in the fire. More than a year later, huddled in a cold office in a Chernihiv detention centre, about two hours north of Kyiv, he barely remembered how he spent the money.
He might have bought a gift for his brother, or maybe he bought himself school supplies.
"You could say I was duped," Vitalii said, describing his situation.
His lawyer is trying to get the charges downgraded to intentional property damage from the more serious charge of sabotage, saying the boys never had any intent to harm Ukraine.
MONEY AS MOTIVATION
Sometimes when teens are arrested and accused of sabotage, the SBU posts images of them on Telegram and Facebook, with their faces blurred.
The posts incite immediate outrage: "It's terrible Ukrainian teenagers are ready to destroy their own country," reads one comment under a January post.
"These idiots! When he gets out of prison he won't be able to live here, people won't forgive!" says another.
In most cases, those charged with arson or sabotage tend to be motivated by money rather than pro-Russian sympathies.
A surge in "credible allegations" that Russia had used Ukrainian children to conduct surveillance and commit sabotage targeting the Ukrainian military was noted by a March 2025 report by the United Nations Office of the High Commissioner for Human Rights.
Last year, a 17-year-old died and a 15-year-old was badly injured when an explosive device they had been instructed to build and take to a drop-off point in western Ukraine exploded.
In March, two police officers were injured after explosions on the outskirts of Kyiv, which the SBU called a terrorist attack orchestrated by Russia.
Authorities arrested a 21-year-old who was recruited online by Russia's intelligence services to build an improvised explosive device, the SBU said.
"Should these incidents be linked to the armed conflict, such use of children would be in violation of the prohibition in international law on the recruitment or use of children in hostilities," the UN report said.
Russia's Federal Security Service did not reply to a request for comment about the online recruitment of minors. A Russian official said Ukraine had repeatedly used Telegram to recruit people for attacks inside Russia.
The SBU did not respond to a request for comment on the Russian allegation, but there have been numerous reports of sabotage within Russia as well.
The office of Ukraine's president did not respond to questions from Reuters.
Recruitment of teen spies is not limited to Ukraine. As reported cases of suspected incidents of Russian sabotage rose all across Europe last year, at least a dozen teens – in Germany, Poland, Britain and Lithuania – have been arrested in Russia-linked cases of sabotage and spying.
The SBU and Ukraine's National Police last year launched an ad campaign to discourage young Ukrainians from working on behalf of Russia.
A video promoted in January 2025 features a young teenage boy in a cap and black hoodie following a Ukrainian soldier returning home from the front, and another girl torching a military vehicle.
At the end of the video, the two teenagers are arrested and put behind bars.
"These crimes are subject to severe criminal liability, even for minors," reads the text posted on YouTube below the video.
The SBU also created a Telegram bot that lets anyone report an acquaintance who has been offered money in exchange for committing sabotage or arson against the state.
As of Feb 2026, Ukraine's Prosecutor General's office said 240 minors were involved in crimes against national security, arson, and terrorist acts related to the possible involvement of Russia's special services or others working in Russia's interest.
Of those, 102 have been detained.
CLASSES IN WARTIME
Hennadiy Yachnyi, a physics teacher from a high school in Chernihiv, contends with children behind bars nearly every day.
On one of the first cold days of autumn last year, Yachnyi passed through five heavy metal prison doors of the pre-trial detention centre in the city, walked up a staircase covered in peeling green paint, and stepped into a small library.
[[nid:1428339]]
Vitalii, who is now 17, soon filed into the room with a group of other teenage detainees. All are minors awaiting trial and sentencing. Only Vitalii and a teen girl are accused of sabotage.
The others face regular criminal charges. Yachnyi commutes to the detention centre several times a week to teach classes behind bars.
"I don't see them as criminals," Yachnyi told Reuters later. "These are students, just students."
Yachnyi is part of a programme that pairs school teachers with minors in detention centres.
The growing number of minors accused of sabotage has led to an extension of the programme for one area high school in particular, which sends a rotating group of teachers to the detention centre to teach lessons ranging from history to mathematics.
Antonina Kharchenko, the director of the school, began sending her teachers to the Chernihiv detention centre in 2023.
They were meant to be part of the programme for two years, but she said the local education department asked her to extend for an additional two years.
None of the teachers are required to go, she said. Though none of the teachers at Kharchenko's school have declined, some teachers at other schools have refused, viewing the children's crimes as unforgivable.
Children looking to make easy money are duped into accepting questionable offers from strangers online, Kharchenko said.
She said parents largely have no idea.
"They all come from Ukrainian families. They decide to earn a penny but they're still children," she said.
"And look around – this is a place with poverty, with war, fathers being away on the front, this is fertile recruiting ground for Russians."
At Chernihiv's regular high school, Kharchenko and her teachers do their best to educate other students and warn them against falling into a similar trap.
A police officer is stationed at the entrance of the school at all times and the SBU regularly speaks to its more than 1,000 students about the dangers of interacting with strangers on social media.
"Children, of course, see war every day," Kharchenko said. "A lot of our children's parents are at the front. There are those who died, there are those whose mother and father died."
Kharchenko's own relatives are serving at the front.
During the first months of the full-scale war, Kharchenko transformed the school's large basement into a bomb shelter.
After Russian troops were pushed back, the school served as a hub for locals to receive medical care and food. Then the basement was converted again into makeshift classrooms.
Over the winter, during daily Russian attacks, the city saw some of Ukraine's worst blackouts, with families going without electricity and heating for weeks in subzero temperatures.
Asked if she feels uncomfortable teaching accused saboteurs when her own graduates are serving and dying at the front line, Kharchenko impatiently pulled a well-worn book from the cupboard of her office.
"This, as you must know, is the constitution of Ukraine," she said, pointing her manicured finger at the blue cover bearing an embossed gold trident, Ukraine's national symbol. Flipping to a dog-eared page, she pointed to Article 53.
"And this clearly states that everyone has the right to an education. What we do is create a possibility for this to be upheld. We're educating children at a time of war.
"We're educating them in shelters, we're educating those online who are abroad, and we're educating the children in the jail as well."
Yachnyi, the physics teacher, was sympathetic towards the young people he teaches at the detention centre and believed they regretted what they'd done.
"I can tell from their communication, how they talk to me, how they wait for me to go to them and talk to them," Yachnyi said in his classroom in the regular Chernihiv high school. Then he ushered his students to their seats to wait out an air raid.
NO EASY MONEY
Vitalii grew up on the edge of a small town in Chernihiv region, which borders both Belarus and Russia to the north and was briefly overrun by Russian soldiers early in the war.
Before the fire, Vitalii mostly stayed inside the family's wooden home, which is on a gravel road near the town cemetery.
"He's a homebody, my son, he doesn't smoke, doesn't drink … He's never even been in a fight before," said Vitalii's mother Nataliia, hugging her arms against the cold outside the house, just a few minutes' walk from the flags that mark the graves of fallen Ukrainian soldiers.
Nataliia picks up odd cleaning jobs around the village and in Kyiv whenever possible. She said her son also started working for a dentist after his father died of kidney cancer six years ago.
Vitalii's youngest brother was often sick, and he wanted to help support his family. She asked that the family name be withheld to avoid being targeted with attacks or harassment.
"We had tried all the work that was available but money had run out," Vitalii said.
In late summer 2024, one of Vitalii's school friends approached him with a job.
According to the indictment filed last March by Chernihiv prosecutors, Vitalii's friend, who is among the boys now charged, had been in touch online with a person offering to pay for specific tasks.
[[nid:1428338]]
At first, one of the boys was asked to set fire to Ukrainian military vehicles, which the boy's lawyer said he did not do.
Later, the lawyer said, the request from someone identifying himself as a man named Sania was to vandalise rail equipment.
Before Sania would pay, he wanted proof – timestamped and geolocated video and photos sent through an app called Timemark.
Vitalii's friend recruited him and his friends, giving them detailed instructions.
A few weeks later, in October 2024, some of the boys got caught by a railway worker as they were trying to set another fire. They quickly confessed and gave the police the names of everyone in the group, including Vitalii.
Vitalii and four other boys were arrested and charged. Vitalii's family was the only one unable to post bond, which equalled around US$6,000.
A year later, he was still in the Chernihiv detention centre while the others were free on bail.
"If I could talk to someone thinking of doing anything like I did, I'd tell them not to," he said quietly, looking at his hands. A guard stood within earshot. "I'd say there's no easy way to make easy money like that."
None of the other boys or their families agreed to speak to Reuters when contacted through their lawyer. Judges in charge of Vitalii's case also declined to speak to Reuters, citing the ongoing trial process.
That day in the centre, Vitalii's face was pale, and there were shadows under his eyes. His hair buzzed short, dressed in black track pants and sliders, he looked small for his age.
If convicted, Vitalii and the other boys face up to a decade in prison, the maximum sentence for their crime. Adults who commit similar crimes can face life terms.
In the detention centre, between classes, Vitalii began reading a memoir of a man who spent several years in an American prison, which taught him the basics of how to survive incarceration including staying fit and keeping the mind occupied.
'THEY'VE MADE A MISTAKE'
Yevheniy Muzychuk, Vitalii's lawyer who also represents two other boys in the case, said Vitalii was never in touch directly with the man called Sania, who pretended to be a Ukrainian patriot on various Telegram groups.
None of the boys – who were 14 and 15 at the time of the alleged crime – could have known they were potentially working for the Russian security services, Muzychuk said.
Identifying an actual Russian recruiter is difficult, and Ukraine's law enforcement sometimes focuses instead on accusing vulnerable teenagers, he said.
Prosecutors and judges should be required to build stronger cases to prove that these teens were knowingly betraying their country, Muzychuk says.
It's not obvious to a civilian, for example, much less a minor, that damaging rail equipment could be tantamount to gravely damaging Ukraine, he says.
The boys have formally made a "partial admission of guilt" for setting the fire, and Muzychuk hopes the judge will accept that they didn't realise that it was sabotage.
"They themselves understand they've made a mistake," he said.
In a written response to Reuters questions, the regional prosecutor's office in Chernihiv said that the prosecution took into account the "totality of evidence" and all of the circumstances of the case, including the fact that the defendants were repeatedly educated on Russia's use of social media to recruit saboteurs before setting the fire.
The prosecutors said the minors were warned, in particular, of Russia's recruitment of teens to carry out sabotage on critical infrastructure in the railway sector.
A separate investigation is underway on "Sania," the individual who recruited the minors, the prosecutor's office said.
In Kyiv, Yevhen Pikalov, Ukraine's deputy justice minister, has the unenviable task of contending with the issue of such young offenders while also reforming a wider prison system.
Pikalov, who was previously part of Ukraine's delegation to the European Union, said the country's penitentiary system had remained "forgotten" for decades.
The detention centre in Chernihiv is a collection of dilapidated concrete blocks, with chipped-paint corridors leading to small, barred cells that detainees share.
Minors like Vitalii are separated from adult detainees until they're convicted, but could serve alongside older inmates after conviction.
Pikalov would not give an opinion about the wartime sentencing of minors, but said Ukrainian society would welcome them back once they have served their time. He himself is the father of two teenagers.
"I think that Ukrainian society understands that when a child is 15 years old, he doesn't understand what he has done. And that matters," he said.
'HE'S FINALLY FREE'
At the end of November, after he'd spent more than a year in the Chernihiv pre-trial detention centre, Vitalii's mother gathered enough money to bail out her son. On the day of his release, Nataliia stood nervously in front of the massive metal gates.
When Vitalii, dressed in black, emerged, Nataliia rushed to the lanky boy and embraced him with a smile: "He's finally free."
Later, back home, Nataliia and her sister stood over bubbling pots, preparing a celebratory feast for Vitalii.
A large Ukrainian flag with signatures and messages from soldiers covers one wall of the kitchen. The flag belonged to Nataliia's partner, who fought against Russian-backed separatist forces in 2014.
"A year and a month," Nataliia says, taking a break from cooking to stand outside in the muddy garden. To cover bail, she took out a bank loan and begged her relatives and friends to pitch in, she says.
Coming up with US$6,000 took up every moment of her days. When she wasn't worried about her son, she worried about money.
"This year was probably the most terrible in my life," she said.
Vitalii's trial is ongoing. His lawyer says he will appeal any conviction, but that process may take months, maybe years. Vitalii's stay at home may be a short reprieve from what comes next.
"I think these boys, they also didn't understand what they were doing," Nataliia said, still defending her son.
Vitalii stood outside in his black sliders with the dog, listening to his mother in silence. With his hands in his pockets, he bowed his head.
* The writers involved in this Special Report are from Reuters
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Mossad chief lauds "groundbreaking" covert operations against Iran, Hizbollah
Article:
JERUSALEM: The head of Israel's Mossad has praised the spy agency's "groundbreaking" operations in the war against Iran and Hizbollah, saying it acquired intelligence "from the heart of the enemy's secrets."
"In the campaigns against Iran and Hizbollah, we worked shoulder-to-shoulder with the IDF, on both defence and offence," David Barnea said, referring to the Israeli military.
His remarks were delivered during a commendation ceremony at Mossad headquarters on Monday and published on Tuesday.
"We acquired strategic and tactical intelligence from the heart of the enemy's secrets," Barnea said, adding that Mossad had "proved new, groundbreaking operational capabilities in target countries."
A ceasefire in the war with Iran, which began with US-Israeli strikes on Feb 28, has so far held.
In Lebanon, Hizbollah drew the country into the war on March 2 by firing rockets towards Israel to avenge the killing of Iranian supreme leader Ali Khamenei by the US and Israel.
US President Donald Trump announced a ceasefire on the Lebanese front earlier this month, which has since been extended.
Despite the truce, Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said Monday that Hizbollah's rockets and drones remained a key threat demanding action by the Israeli military, adding that Israel was continuing to carry out strikes.
Hizbollah has said it is responding to Israeli ceasefire "violations."
Barnea said the military and Mossad had changed Israel's "strategic posture" and "strengthened its might", but added that the agency would "not rest on our laurels."
"When we see a threat, we will act with full force," he said.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Luxury gifts, market manipulation: South Korea's former first lady hit with tougher sentence
Article:
SEOUL: A South Korean appeals court increased the corruption sentence for former first lady Kim Keon Hee on Tuesday to four years in jail, up from 20 months, after finding her guilty of stock manipulation and bribery.
The much heavier penalty came as the court overturned Kim's prior acquittal by a lower court on stock price manipulation charges.
"The court sentences the defendant to four years in prison and imposes a 50 million won (US$34,000) fine," the Seoul High Court said in a verdict televised live.
It found Kim guilty of manipulating the share price of Deutsch Motors, a South Korean car dealer, which it ruled a "collusive... trading act constituting market manipulation."
"The defendant appears to have participated in such conduct," the court said as it overturned her initial acquittal.
Kim, the wife of jailed ex-president Yoon Suk Yeol, was handed 20 months in jail in January for bribery after she accepted luxury gifts from a cult-like church.
She appealed the verdict in the hope of clearing her name, while prosecutors also did so arguing that the sentence was too lenient and that her acquittals were wrongful.
The court said on Tuesday that the 53-year-old had "failed to acknowledge her culpability and has instead consistently resorted to excuses."
As a result of Kim's acceptance of bribes, "public trust in the transparency of state affairs and the fair execution of national policy was undermined," the court said.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Cleaner fined RM1,500 over ex-girlfriend's obscene video
Article:
MUAR: A cleaner was fined RM1,500 by the magistrate's court here today after pleading guilty to possessing and distributing an obscene video of his ex-girlfriend last week.
Magistrate Kartini Kasran meted out the sentence to Muhammad Fauzi Helmy Mohd Noh, 29, under Section 292 of the Penal Code.
The court also ordered him to serve four months' imprisonment if he failed to pay the fine. He settled the fine.
According to the charge, the accused was found in possession of an obscene video of his former girlfriend on his mobile phone.
The offence was committed at the Muar district police headquarters' Commercial Crime Investigation Division office about 3.30pm on April 22.
The charge carries a maximum penalty of three years' imprisonment, a fine, or both, upon conviction.
Based on the facts of the case, the 37-year-old victim lodged a police report after a colleague informed her that they had received the video from the accused.
Investigations revealed that the video was recorded by the accused without the victim's knowledge during an intimate encounter.
Acting on the report, police arrested the accused and seized his mobile phone for examination. A forensic inspection uncovered a file containing the obscene footage.
Deputy public prosecutor Yasmin Mohd Piah conducted the prosecution, while the accused was represented by Sakinah Suhaimi from the National Legal Aid Foundation.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Proof of death: Man exhumes sister's remains after bank refusal
Article:
KEONJHAR: A man exhumed his sister's skeletal remains and staged a protest outside a rural bank in Odisha after failing to withdraw money from her account.
The incident occurred about 1pm on Monday at a rural bank in Patana block, Keonjhar.
The man, identified as Jitu Munda, 50, from Malipasi village, had been attempting to withdraw about Rs19,300 (RM806.47) from the account of his elder sister, who died on Jan 26.
According to the Times of India, reports said Jitu had visited the bank several times but was unable to access the funds due to procedural requirements, including documentation to prove his sister's death.
However, being illiterate and unable to navigate the bureaucratic process, he took the request for "proof of death" literally.
He reportedly exhumed his sister's remains and presented them at the bank counter as evidence.
The act alarmed bank staff and members of the public, prompting officials to alert the police.
Patana police station inspector-in-charge Kiran Prasad Sahu said the man had not acted out of malice but due to a lack of understanding.
"He is innocent and could not understand the process of obtaining a death certificate to be submitted to the bank," he said.
Police later intervened and persuaded Jitu to return the remains to their resting place.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Two foreigners arrested for smuggling petrol in modified tanks near border
Article:
PADANG TERAP: Two foreign women were arrested and their cars seized near the border in Durian Burung yesterday after enforcement officers found additional petrol tanks installed in their vehicles.
Kedah Domestic Trade and Cost of Living Ministry chief enforcement officer Zalina Aziz said the suspects, in their 30s and 40s, were intercepted by the General Operations Force (GOF) at 2pm.
The arrests occurred at a roadblock in front of the Kota Putra police station before the suspects were handed over to the ministry's officers for further action.
"Inspections revealed both cars, which were locally registered, were carrying controlled items in a manner that created reasonable suspicion that an offence was being committed," she said in a statement today.
Authorities seized about 200 litres of petrol worth RM774 and two Proton Wiras. The total value of the seizure, including the vehicles, is estimated at RM15,574.
Zalina added that both suspects are being investigated under the Control of Supplies Act 1961.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ex-policeman pleads guilty to impersonating officer to steal cigarettes
Article:
BATU PAHAT: A former policeman today pleaded guilty at the Yong Peng magistrate's court to impersonating a civil servant to steal a pack of cigarettes from a convenience store last week.
Airullah Fazlemoula, 45, who has a criminal record for theft, entered the plea to two charges before magistrate Arun Noval Dass.
The court, however, postponed sentencing and ordered the accused to undergo psychiatric evaluation at Permai Hospital in Johor Baru on May 25.
The evaluation was ordered under Section 342 of the Criminal Procedure Code to allow the court to review a medical report before sentencing.
On the first charge, the accused was alleged to have pretended to be a police officer despite no longer holding any position in the force.
He was charged under Section 170 of the Penal Code, which carries a jail term of up to two years, a fine, or both.
For the second charge, he was accused of dishonestly stealing a pack of cigarettes under Section 380 of the Penal Code.
The offences were committed at a convenience store in Jalan Ismail, Senggarang, at 11.10pm on April 21.
According to the facts of the case, a store employee lodged a police report after the accused arrived at the premises clad in a police uniform.
The accused asked for a pack of cigarettes, and when asked to pay, he claimed a friend would settle the bill later.
When pressed for payment again, the accused said, "Tell your boss to show some respect for this uniform", before driving away.
Police arrested him at a house in Jalan Benut, Senggarang, at 4.25am the following day.
Prosecuting officer Inspector Aizuddin Sulaiman appeared for the prosecution, while the accused was represented by Tan Song Yan from the National Legal Aid Foundation.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Iran: US no longer in position to 'dictate' policy to other nations
Article:
TEHERAN: Iran said on Tuesday that the United States was no longer able to "dictate" what other countries do, as Washington weighed a new proposal from Tehran on ending the Middle East war.
"The United States is no longer in a position to dictate its policy to independent nations," defence ministry spokesman Reza Talaei-Nik said, according to state TV, adding Washington would "accept that it must abandon its illegal and irrational demands."--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Part-time driver charged with trafficking over half a tonne of MDMA
Article:
MELAKA: A part-time driver was charged at the Ayer Keroh magistrate's court here on five charges of trafficking 569.36kg of methylenedioxymethamphetamine (MDMA) in March.
Chai Koh Chun, 55, nodded in understanding when the charges were read to him in front of magistrate Sharda Shienha Mohd Suleiman.
No plea was recorded as the case falls under the jurisdiction of the High Court.
Under the first charge, the accused allegedly trafficked 269,382.78g of MDMA, and he is accused of trafficking the same drug with a gross weight of 298,650g under the second charge, while the remaining two charges relate to trafficking 176.42g and 150.88g of MDMA and 1,003.30g of ketamine.
The offences allegedly took place at a house along Jalan Samarinda 5, Taman Satu Krubong, Krubong, in Melaka Tengah district around 4pm on March 8.
The charges were submitted under Section 39B(1)(a) of the Dangerous Drugs Act 1952 punishable under Section 39B(2) of the same, which carries the death penalty or life imprisonment and no less than 12 lashes if convicted.
Deputy public prosecutor Syaza Nur Sharif did not offer any bail, while lawyers S. Paul Krishnaraja and Chua Yong Yi represented the accused.
The court did not allow bail and set June 18 for the submission of the chemicals report.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Melaka scam syndicate offered RM2,500 – RM6,000 salaries plus commissions, say police
Article:
MELAKA: Police have crippled a fraud syndicate involved in love scams, non-existent investments, and sex services scams following raids in Taman Kota Bendahari, Klebang.
State police chief Datuk Dzulkhairi Mukhtar said the simultaneous raids were conducted by the state Commercial Crime Investigation Department (CCID), Melaka Tengah district CCID, and the state Special Branch at 11am on April 23.
He said 45 foreigners aged between 20 and 44 were arrested.
They comprise 20 Chinese men, 19 Vietnamese men, four Vietnamese women, one Chinese woman, and one Indonesian woman.
"Initial investigations found the love scam and investment syndicate had been active for four months, targeting Vietnamese nationals.
"They used the Rakuten Viber application to entice victims with sweet talk.
"After gaining their trust, victims were offered cryptocurrency investments on fake websites," he said at a press conference at the Melaka police headquarters today.
Dzulkhairi said syndicate members were paid between RM2,500 and RM5,000 monthly, while supervisors received between RM5,000 and RM6,000, depending on their performance.
"They were also promised commissions of between RM500 and RM2,000 for every victim successfully duped," he said.
The second raid targeted an online syndicate offering non-existent sex services to Chinese nationals through the DingTalk application using prepared scripts.
Items seized from both premises included three laptops, 21 desktop computers, 85 mobile phones, three modems and routers, 18 scam script documents, and 32 passports.
All suspects have been remanded until April 30.
The case is being investigated under Section 420 of the Penal Code, read together with Section 120B, as well as several offences under the Immigration Act 1959/63, he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Two men charged as mule account proxies in online scams
Article:
JOHOR BARU: Two men were charged in the Sessions Court here today with acting as account mules for online sales and investment scam syndicates.
Azlan Shah Shahrim Effendy, 30, and Muhammad Dafy Irfan Mohd Asmadi, 19, pleaded not guilty after the charges were read separately before judges Mohd Zamir Suhaimee and Datuk Che Wan Zaidi Che Wan Ibrahim.
Azlan Shah, who is unemployed, was accused of using a bank account registered under the name Huatai Wholesale Trading to receive transactions amounting to RM51,000 without a legitimate purpose.
The offence was allegedly committed at Horizon Hills, Iskandar Puteri, between May 8 and May 12 last year.
He was charged under Section 424C(1) of the Penal Code, which carries a fine of up to RM150,000, a maximum prison term of 10 years, or both, upon conviction.
Meanwhile, Muhammad Dafy Irfan, a food stall assistant, faced an alternative charge of dishonestly assisting in the transfer of RM700 belonging to a man into his own bank account.
The offence was allegedly committed at Jalan Nusa Bestari 9/8, Taman Nusa Bestari, Iskandar Puteri, on Dec 31 last year.
He was charged under Section 424 of the Penal Code, which provides for a maximum five-year jail term, a fine, or both, upon conviction.
Deputy public prosecutors Nurul Syafiqah Shaari and Nur Diyana Zubir appeared for the prosecution, while both accused were unrepresented.
The court allowed bail of RM3,000 with one local surety for Muhammad Dafy Irfan. No bail was granted to Azlan Shah, as he is currently serving a prison sentence in a separate case.
The court fixed May 4 and June 4 for mention of the respective cases.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ramanan: RM9.24mil allocated to 462 temples under Dharma Madani
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A total of RM9.24 million has been channelled to 462 temples nationwide under the Dharma Madani programme, said Human Resources Minister Datuk Seri R. Ramanan.
He said the first phase, which began on Jan 29, involved 162 temples with an allocation of nearly RM3.24 million.
The second phase begins today, involving RM6 million for 300 temples.
"The programme will continue into the third and fourth phases, but several applications are still incomplete in terms of documentation," he told reporters after handing over approval letters to 300 temples today.
Also present were Human Resources Ministry deputy secretary-general (policy and international) Dr Mohd Shaharin Umar and Malaysian Indian Transformation Unit (Mitra) director-general Raveendran Nair.
Ramanan said Mitra has been instructed to assist in completing the required documentation to ensure applications are processed smoothly.
"There are several temples with incomplete applications, and additional documents are still required. We will monitor and provide guidance, and once everything is in order, approvals will be granted.
"We do not reject applications; we will assist all applicants and grant approval once all conditions are met," he said.
On a separate matter, Ramanan said efforts to upgrade staffing positions at Mitra are under consideration and awaiting formal approval, having already been agreed upon at the policy level.
He said additional positions are crucial for Mitra to establish a one-stop centre for the Indian community to access information on various government initiatives.
"Through this centre, the community will have easier access to information and benefits offered, including programmes such as Vetri Madani under HRD Corp, which is still not widely known," he said. — BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: May 5 EGM to decide future of Saujana Golf and Country Club
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Saujana Resort (M) Bhd will ask shareholders to approve several proposals at an adjourned extraordinary general meeting (EGM) on May 5.
The proposals are intended to boost revenue and ensure the long-term sustainability of Saujana Golf and Country Club.
In a circular to shareholders, the company said the proposals are intended to enlarge the club's membership, with the objective of improving recurring income.
"With stronger revenue, there is a real opportunity to reduce the current subscription rate, rather than increase it," it said.
Saujana Resort said the revenue from about 1,450 members, out of 3,800 eligible shares, paying RM737.10 per month, has been insufficient to meet operating costs and capital expenditure.
It noted that the club has operated at a loss for several years and has relied on financial support of about RM70 million from its controlling shareholder, Peremba (Malaysia) Sdn Bhd, which has indicated it does not intend to extend further funding.
To address this, Saujana Resort is proposing the issuance of 7,600 redeemable preference shares (RPS) to expand membership and place the company on a more sustainable financial footing.
"If approved, the company will have a single class of ordinary shares. All shareholders will retain their ownership and proportional interest," it said.
Saujana Resort said the proposals could increase the membership base to 7,600 while allowing monthly subscriptions to be reduced to not more than RM450, excluding service tax.
The board has carefully considered these proposals as a pathway to unify shareholders, strengthen the club's financial position and maintain its standing as a premier golf and lifestyle destination, it added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Teenagers detained for trespassing on LRT tracks to dance
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Police detained two teenagers after one of them deliberately trespassed onto the tracks at the Muhibbah LRT station and danced on the railway line on Saturday.
Cheras police chief Assistant Commissioner Mohd Rosdi Daud said the incident, which occurred at 11.52pm, was detected by security personnel through closed-circuit television (CCTV) footage, where one teenager was seen intentionally getting onto the tracks at Platform 1 solely to dance for entertainment purposes.
"The teenagers, aged 16 and 17, were detained by auxiliary policemen before being handed over to police from the Bukit Jalil station for further action.
"They were remanded for two days until Sunday and were later released on police bail," he said in a statement.
Rosdi said the case is being investigated under Section 62 of the Railways Act 1991 for trespassing on railway tracks.
If convicted, offenders may face a fine or imprisonment.
Rosdi said the investigation papers were briefed to the deputy public prosecutor today, who instructed that the teenagers be released.
He said this as they are juveniles and continued detention could affect their future.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Johor tops Malaysia's a leading data centre hub
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Johor has cemented its position as Malaysia's main data centre growth engine, with JLL Malaysia projecting the southern state to remain the country's dominant digital infrastructure hub as hyperscale operators accelerate expansion.
The property consultancy firm said Johor is now the centre of Malaysia's data centre boom, leading the country's rapid capacity expansion on the back of its proximity to Singapore, lower development costs and growing appeal to global technology players.
JLL director of capital markets and data centres Keith Eng said Johor now has 850 megawatts of operational data centre capacity, with another 1,800 megawatts under construction and 2,700 megawatts in the pipeline, making it by far the largest and fastest-growing data centre cluster in Malaysia.
This places Johor well ahead of Greater Kuala Lumpur, which remains an important secondary market but is significantly smaller in scale, with 182 megawatts operational, 615 megawatts under construction and 785 megawatts planned.
Eng said Johor's rise reflects a structural shift in how regional digital infrastructure is being built, with operators increasingly turning to the state as the most viable alternative to land- and power-constrained Singapore.
Nusajaya has emerged as Johor's premier data centre destination, driven by its proximity to Singapore via the Tuas Second Link and its ability to deliver low-latency connectivity for Singapore-dependent workloads.
Meanwhile, Sedenak has developed into one of Southeast Asia's largest purpose-built data centre hubs, with dedicated power and water infrastructure designed specifically for hyperscale operators. The area has become a key anchor for large-scale digital infrastructure deployment, with its first phase fully taken up and a second phase expected to launch soon.
Kulai is also gaining momentum as another major growth node, supported by YTL Group's planned 500-megawatt green data centre campus, which is expected to house international technology players including Sea Ltd and Nvidia.
According to JLL, Johor Baru has recorded the fastest co-location growth in Asia-Pacific over the past five years, expanding 5,262 per cent since 2020 and snatching the highest growth rate in the region.
In absolute terms, Johor added 574 megawatts over the same period, placing it among the top three fastest-growing data centre markets in Asia-Pacific alongside Tokyo and Beijing.
Eng said Johor's rapid expansion is being driven by rising demand from artificial intelligence, cloud computing, 5G and enterprise digitalisation, all of which are increasing the need for hyperscale and colocation capacity.
He added that Malaysia's total operational data centre capacity is expected to more than double from 900 megawatts at end-2025 to 2,055 megawatts by end-2026, with Johor accounting for the bulk of that growth.
This positions Johor not just as Malaysia's largest data centre market but also as the state driving the country's emergence as one of Southeast Asia's fastest-growing digital infrastructure hubs.
Johor's next phase of growth will depend increasingly on infrastructure readiness, particularly access to power.
Grid connection delays are emerging as the biggest bottleneck for operators across the region, with power availability now becoming a more critical determinant of site selection than land cost alone.
As a result, operators are increasingly exploring secondary corridors such as Tebrau, Ulu Tiram and Pasir Gudang, where utility access may offer greater flexibility for future expansion.
The shift is likely to define Johor's next phase of growth, as developers look beyond core clusters to secure power capacity and integrate renewable energy solutions to support long-term expansion.
Despite these constraints, JLL said Johor is the best place to lead Malaysia's next wave of data centre growth, with scale, connectivity and cost advantages continuing to place it at the centre of the country's digital infrastructure expansion.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Three teens held over sexual assault against 14-year-old girl in Tawau
Article:
TAWAU: Three teenagers, including two minors, have been arrested for alleged sexual crimes against a 14-year-old schoolgirl in several incidents since late last year.
District police chief Assistant Commissioner Jasmin Hussin said the arrests followed a police report lodged last Saturday regarding incidents that allegedly occurred between November last year and February.
Following the report, police investigations led to the detention of the three suspects.
Two suspects, aged 14 and 17, were arrested at 11.50am yesterday in Kampung Jawa.
Another 17-year-old was detained two hours later at the Tawau district police headquarters.
"Initial investigations found the victim was allegedly involved in several incidents with different suspects, including at a house and public premises," he said in a statement today.
Jasmin said police are also investigating another individual in a separate incident at a school, where the victim was allegedly forced to perform an indecent act.
The case is being investigated under Section 376(1) of the Penal Code and Section 14(a) of the Sexual Offences Against Children Act 2017.
"All suspects are currently in custody to assist with investigations. We are completing the investigation papers before referring the matter to the prosecution," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Family of late cook donate his organs in final act of kindness [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA TERENGGANU: The family of the late Muhammad Syukri Bazli hopes his organ donation will offer a second chance at life for those in need.
His uncle, Azlan Yahya, 58, said the decision was made to donate his organs on Sunday with the consent of Syukri's parents and his wife, Nurul Hanis Amelia Ramli, 32.
Azlan described the donation as a final act of kindness for the 31-year-old cook, whom he remembered as a patient and helpful individual.
"The decision was made as he was still young, and there are others in need of life-saving help. He never spoke about organ donation before, but knowing his character, the family felt this was the right decision," he said.
Syukri, who worked at a restaurant in Kuala Berang, died of heart complications at Hospital Sultanah Nur Zahirah (HSNZ) at 12.06am. The organ procurement process began at 5am.
The donated organs and tissues include the kidneys, liver, bones, and skin for burn victims and reconstructive surgery.
"We will miss him, especially during gatherings, as he was always helpful and warm," Azlan said, adding that the family appreciated the care provided by HSNZ staff.
Earlier, a video of HSNZ staff performing a Walk of Honour for Syukri went viral on social media, a gesture held in recognition of his contribution.
The Terengganu Health Department also shared a message of appreciation on its official Facebook page, thanking the donor, his family, and the healthcare personnel involved.
View on Threads
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Muhyiddin trial: Bank flagged inflow of RM19.5m into Bersatu's account, court told [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The inflow of RM19.5 million into Bersatu's account from a construction company was "out of the ordinary", the High Court heard today.
Bank Muamalat Malaysia Shah Alam branch assistant manager Norazeanti Miswan said the sum was transferred from Mamfor Sdn Bhd's account between May 12, 2021 and Nov 16, 2022.
She was testifying in the case against former prime minister Tan Sri Muhyiddin Yassin, who faces seven charges linked to the Jana Wibawa project.
He is accused of four counts of abuse of power involving RM225.3 million in alleged gratification for Bersatu and three counts of money laundering involving RM200 million.
"To my knowledge, Mamfor did not make contributions to other political parties, such as Umno, DAP and Pas," Norazeanti said.
In response to deputy public prosecutor Datuk Wan Shaharuddin Wan Ladin, Norazeanti said the bank issued a Suspicious Transaction Report (STR) over 29 transactions linked to the company.
Wan Shaharuddin:
So, was a Suspicious Transaction Report (STR) issued by the bank?
Norazeanti:
Yes, it is for any suspicious activity. In this case, 29 transactions were involved. The STR was issued and submitted to Bank Muamalat's headquarters.
Wan Shaharuddin:
Why?
Norazeanti:
There were doubts over the transactions by Mamfor as the withdrawals were large and occurred frequently.
Wan Shaharuddin:
What do you mean by frequent and large?
Norazeanti:
Bank cheque withdrawals ranged between RM500,000 and RM2 million, occurring multiple times from 2021 until the end of 2022.
Wan Shaharuddin:
In your experience as a bank officer, are such transactions common for a company?
Norazeanti:
No, I have never seen such transactions by other companies to political parties, as payments are usually in line with the nature of their business.
She told the court that Mamfor's account was closed on Feb 26, 2025, after the bank issued a notice for its closure.
The trial before judge Noor Ruwena Md Nurdin continues.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Anwar: Higher education success defined by quality, not just numbers
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia's standing as a regional education hub must be judged by the calibre of its graduates rather than mere enrolment figures, said Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim.
The prime minister, in noting that the country is currently a top choice for over 160,000 international students, emphasised that true success lies in nurturing individuals who are knowledgeable, ethical and competitive.
Speaking at the official launch of UCSI University's campus expansion and the 'Temu Anwar' session today, he called on local institutions to uphold high standards of quality and integrity to remain globally respected.
"Our responsibility goes beyond attracting foreign students or boasting about having the highest statistics in the Asean region... the more vital task is ensuring that the quality of human capital we develop aligns with the nation's aspirations." he said in a social media posting
Anwar also highlighted the need to balance rapid technological growth with human values.
He cautioned that as the country drives progress in new technologies, the "human dimension" cannot be sidelined.
"Values, ethics, and morality must remain our core. This ensures that technological progress is balanced with the development of a strong, principled character and identity," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] RM7.7mil worth of unregistered weight-loss drugs seized at KLIA
Article:
SEPANG: The Malaysia Border Control and Protection Agency (MCBA) has foiled two separate attempts to smuggle unregistered pharmaceutical products through Kuala Lumpur International Airport (KLIA), with total seizures valued at some RM7.7 million.
More than 12,000 syringes believed to contain tirzepatide — a drug used to treat type 2 diabetes and sleep apnoea, and increasingly misused for weight loss — were seized in the operations.
KLIA MCBA commander Mohd Safrie Zakaria said the interceptions, carried out at Terminal 1 and Terminal 2 on consecutive days, underscored the agency's vigilance in safeguarding the country's entry points.
He said the operations also reflected close coordination with key agencies, including the Health Ministry, to curb the entry of hazardous and unregistered medical products.
"In the first case, a man from China who arrived from Bangladesh was stopped at Terminal 1 at about 3.30am on April 26.
"Inspection of his luggage led to the discovery of 3,300 units of injectable drugs labelled 'Tirzide', believed to contain tirzepatide, a controlled substance listed under the Poisons Act 1952, and which is not registered in Malaysia," he told reporters at the KLIA Airport Management Centre today.
The seized items were estimated to be worth RM2.33 million.
"In the second case, a man and a woman from China, who also arrived from Bangladesh, were intercepted at Terminal 2 about 5.30am on April 27.
"A total of 8,840 units of injectable drugs suspected to be unregistered were found in the woman's luggage. The products are believed to contain tirzepatide and were labelled 'Tirzide' and 'Tirzep'," he said.
The second seizure was valued at about RM5.4 million.
Safrie said both cases involved unusually large quantities of injectable medication, which raised suspicion during baggage screening.
"In legitimate use, such medication is typically carried in small quantities. However, in these cases, the luggage contained only the items in bulk, which is highly suspicious," he said.
He added that while the drugs were intended for diabetes treatment, any pharmaceutical product entering Malaysia must be registered and approved by the Health Ministry.
Both cases are being investigated under the Control of Drugs and Cosmetics Regulations 1984, the Sale of Drugs Act 1952 and the Poisons Act 1952, including offences involving the importation of unregistered products and controlled substances without a valid licence.
Safrie said the seized items had been handed over to the Health Ministry's Pharmaceutical Services Division for further action, as MCBA serves as the frontline enforcement agency at entry points.
"We ensure that any unregistered or potentially harmful products do not enter the local market. Once detected, the cases are referred to the relevant authorities for further investigation," he said.
He said the drugs were typically sold legitimately for between RM1,500 and RM1,800 per multi-dose auto-injector pen.
"When sold illegally, they can fetch between RM400 and RM800 per syringe," he said, adding that the seized products were believed to have been manufactured in Bangladesh.
He said the three people initially detained claimed they were merely in transit and intended to take the items back to China. However, investigations revealed that their return flights were scheduled more than 30 days after their arrival in Malaysia.
Safrie issued a stern warning to those attempting to circumvent Malaysian laws.
"We will not compromise with any party that challenges the law. MCBA will continue to tighten screening at all major entry points to safeguard national security and public health," he said.
He added that flights from high-risk routes, including Bangladesh, were subject to enhanced screening based on profiling and intelligence.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Bursa Malaysia ends at intraday high on improved local sentiment
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, April 28 — Bursa Malaysia rose 0.71 per cent to end at the day’s high on the back of improving local sentiment and selective buying in index-linked counters following the recent pullback.
At 5pm, the FTSE Bursa Malaysia KLCI (FBM KLCI) advanced 12.33 points to 1,729.60 from yesterday’s close of 1,717.27.
The benchmark index, which opened 4.13 points higher at 1,721.40, hit a low of 1,714.12 in the mid-morning session before gaining momentum for the rest of the day toward closing.
Rakuten Trade Sdn Bhd vice-president of equity research Thong Pak Leng said regional markets were mixed as focus remained on the West Asia conflict and upcoming US megacap earnings.
Market breadth was marginally positive, with gainers edging out losers 629 to 626. A total of 530 counters were unchanged, 933 untraded, and 86 suspended.
Turnover inched up to 3.85 billion units worth RM3.38 billion compared with 3.74 billion units valued at RM3.30 billion yesterday.
Thong told Bernama that the FBM KLCI is holding above the 1,700 level and rebounding from around 1,680 last week, suggesting that investors are beginning to accumulate on dips, although the upside may be capped as the index tests the 1,730 resistance zone.
He said that external risks continue to linger as developments in West Asia remain fluid, with peace talks yet to reach a clear resolution, while tensions around the Strait of Hormuz continue to keep oil prices elevated.
Meanwhile, IPPFA Sdn Bhd director of investment strategy and country economist Mohd Sedek Jantan said gains were primarily supported by petrochemical stocks, tracking Brent crude prices, which rose above US$110 per barrel.
At the same time, the FBM70 continued to outperform, highlighting a clear internal rotation within the domestic market, he said.
“While this may appear as an improvement in risk appetite, the underlying pattern suggests a more defensive rotation in nature, where rising global oil prices and persistent geopolitical tensions are driving flows into energy-linked beneficiaries and domestically resilient sectors, rather than signalling a broad-based cyclical recovery,” he added.
Among heavyweights, Maybank rose 12 sen to RM11.22, Public Bank added six sen to RM4.78, CIMB improved 13 sen to RM7.78, IHH Healthcare gained four sen to RM8.81, while Tenaga Nasional was six sen lower at RM14.52.
On the most active list, Luster Industries eased by half-a-sen to 3.5 sen, Zetrix AI fell one sen to 86 sen, VS Industry dropped 1.5 sen to 21 sen, while UEM Sunrise rose 7.5 sen to 66.5 sen, and MRCB bagged two sen to 33.5 sen.
Top gainers included Nestle, which garnered RM3.18 to RM102.30, Fraser & Neave advanced 72 sen to RM31.40, KESM surged 46 sen to RM4.19, United Plantations climbed 38 sen to RM32.00, and Dutch Lady perked up 28 sen to RM32.78.
As for top losers, Malaysian Pacific Industries, which shaved off 38 sen to RM37.50, Allianz decreased 36 sen to RM20.80, Kelington and Batu Kawan slipped 16 sen each to RM6.22 and RM20.98, respectively, and UWC lost 15 sen to RM5.12. — Bernama

Title: Tabung Haji urges Malaysian Haj pilgrims to focus on worship, limit social media use
Article:
MECCA, April 28 — Amid the excitement of sharing moments in the Holy Land, Haj pilgrims are reminded not to let social media distract them from the primary purpose of their pilgrimage.
Lembaga Tabung Haji (TH) Haj Guidance Operations director Shahrin Awaluddin said the trend, particularly among younger pilgrims, could affect the devotion and completeness of their haj rituals.
“When we are in the Holy Land, whether in Makkah or Madinah, we must devote our full attention to performing the rituals in their entirety, not carry them out half-heartedly.
“Haj is not merely a journey, but an act of worship that demands full concentration in drawing closer to Allah SWT,” he told reporters here.
He said the use of social media is not prohibited, but excessive use could cause pilgrims to miss important moments of worship such as
tawaf
,
sa’i
and especially
wuquf
in Arafah.
Meanwhile, Shahrin assured that TH continuously monitors the overall welfare of Malaysian pilgrims throughout their stay in the Holy Land.
He said various facilities are provided, including accommodation, transportation, healthcare services and guidance on performing the rituals, to ensure pilgrims can carry out their obligations properly.
“If pilgrims fall ill or encounter difficulties, our personnel will assist to ensure their Haj can still be performed,” he said.
He also advised family members back home not to worry, as every pilgrim is closely monitored in terms of welfare, health and worship.
The 1447H/2026M Haj operations officially began on April 18, involving 31,600 Malaysian pilgrims departing in stages to Madinah and Makkah via chartered flights operated by AMAL by Malaysia Airlines and Saudia Airlines. — Bernama

Title: Melor rep probe: Kelantan police to submit IP to DPP after statement over viral video mocking officers
Article:
KOTA BHARU, April 28 — Police will submit the investigation paper (IP) involving Melor assemblyman Wan Rohimi Wan Daud to the Deputy Public Prosecutor in the near future after his statement was recorded yesterday.
Kelantan police chief Datuk Mohd Yusoff Mamat said Wan Rohimi gave good cooperation throughout the statement-recording process conducted at the Kota Bharu district police headquarters (IPD).
He said the statements obtained so far are satisfactory and will form part of the investigation before being referred to the prosecution.
“Wan Rohimi attended the Kota Bharu IPD yesterday to give his statement regarding the issue linked to him.
“The police are also expected to call other witnesses who were present when he delivered a brief speech at the event, if necessary,” he told Bernama today.
